ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for clear-braised radish and lamb soup; FOODON_00002809 (lamb soup) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this composite soup dish; A0CKP (sheep meat, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Chinese lamb and radish soup preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of clear-braised radish and lamb soup is approximately 500 g, comprising broth, lamb pieces, and radish chunks in a large bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (Goat/Lamb), cooked, composite — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with Chinese Radish (Daikon) boiled values from USDA FoodData Central; proportional estimation for composite soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for 清炖萝卜羊肉汤 exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a proportional recipe model: approximately 35% cooked lamb (lean, from leg, roasted — NDB 17290), 20% white radish/daikon (boiled — NDB 11429), and 45% clear broth (water with dissolved minerals and fat from simmering). Per-100g values represent the composite edible soup including broth. Energy and macros are consistent with published values for similar Chinese clear lamb soups in peer-reviewed dietary assessment literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant meat-based soups; analogous estimate based on lamb stew / meat broth entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 清炖萝卜羊肉汤. GI is estimated at 25 based on analogous protein- and fat-dominant meat soups and broths in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the 20–35 range. The low carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100g) and high protein and fat content strongly attenuate glycemic response. Daikon radish has a very low GI (~10–15 per Sydney GI Database). Composite GI estimated at 25, gi_category = low (≤55). Glycemic load per 500g serving = (25 × 9.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 500g restaurant serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, which represents 46–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1500–2300 mg) for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100g and an estimated GI of 25, this soup has a negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 2.3 per serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and moderate sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb contributes approximately 1.4 g saturated fat per 100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean lamb cuts, skim visible fat from the broth, and be mindful of the sodium content, limiting intake to one moderate serving per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 清炖萝卜羊肉汤 (Qīng dùn luóbo yángròu tāng), known in English as Clear-Braised Radish and Lamb Soup, is a classic Northern Chinese slow-simmered soup originating from the culinary traditions of northern and northwestern China, including Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering bone-in or boneless lamb cuts with white radish (daikon), fresh ginger, and spring onion in water for one to two hours until the broth becomes clear, fragrant, and deeply flavoured. The resulting soup is characterised by a clean, mildly savoury broth, tender lamb with a slightly gamey depth, and soft radish that absorbs the meat's flavour while contributing a gentle sweetness. The dish is minimally processed and contains no thickeners, heavy sauces, or artificial additives, making it one of the purer expressions of Chinese slow-cooking technique. Nutritionally, the soup is high in protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100g), moderate in fat (3.2 g per 100g), and very low in carbohydrates (1.8 g per 100g), yielding approximately 62 kcal per 100g. It provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus from the lamb, and vitamin C from the radish. The glycemic index is estimated at 25 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium from the broth, which can be significant in restaurant preparations. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is commonly served as a warming main course in cold weather and is associated with restorative and nourishing properties in traditional Chinese dietary culture. In Singapore, it is served at Northern Chinese specialty restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 清炖萝卜羊肉汤 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a proportional recipe model based on cooked lamb and boiled daikon components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is sound and the source data is from an approved primary source, but the composite recipe proportions are approximated.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Meat and alternatives' as this is a meat-based soup dish. 2. Cuisine tags in input included 'Noodle' which is incorrect for this dish; this tag has been disregarded. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimation. 4. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA component data and are marked accordingly. 5. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100g reflecting naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid in ruminant lamb fat, not industrial trans fat. 6. Sodium value of 210 mg per 100g reflects a moderately salted restaurant-style preparation; home preparations may be lower. 7. The restaurant Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay, Singapore) serves this dish at SGD 36.80, consistent with a large shared-portion restaurant serving. 8. GI category is low (GI = 25, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). 9. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the source database for this estimated composite.
